title: Adventurer's Field Notes
author: Anonymous Adventurer
variants:
If you're reading this, you found my chest. Take what's useful.

Three things I wish someone had told me:
1. The slimes don't drop loot worth the swing if you're at half hearts. Walk past.
2. Pots aren't always empty. They aren't always full either. Break them all anyway.
3. Don't sleep on the train. I don't know what happens. I just know I haven't met anyone who has.
Trade emeralds for food before you trade them for anything else. I learned that one late.

I've been keeping a count of carriages I've cleared. I'm not going to tell you the number, because the number doesn't matter and it'll only depress you.

Just keep going. The one after the bad one is usually fine.
If the librarian seems too friendly, he's the same librarian as the last one. They all are. I have no proof of this. I just know it.

This chest used to be a barrel. I broke it down in the dining car and put it back together as a chest because I needed somewhere to leave the things I couldn't carry.

That sentence is tidier than the six hours it actually took. If the lid still closes properly, please appreciate it for a moment before you take everything.

Found this empty in a corner. Decided it deserved to be useful.

I'm not the first one who put something in it, and I won't be the last. We don't talk to each other, us adventurers, but we leave things in the same boxes. That counts as a kind of talking, I think.

Everything in here is on purpose.

I picked the items I think you'll need most, based on what I needed most. I'm probably wrong about half of them. But I tried.

I hid this one.

The chests I left in the open kept getting emptied by something I never saw. Don't tell me what you think it was. I don't want to know.

Take what you want. Replace it with something useful if you can.

There was already a book in this chest when I found it. I read it. It was someone else's notes for the next adventurer. I'm leaving it in here too.

You might be holding mine. You might be holding theirs. I can't actually remember which one I'm writing in right now.

This is my chest. Took me three carriages to find the planks.

Take whatever you need. Try not to take all of it.

You'll read this once. Just so you know.

Whatever book you pick up here, however carefully you treat it — once you've read it, it burns. Pages first, then the binding, then the smell of paper, in that order.

I've gotten used to it. I don't promise you will.

My theory on the burning books — and you'll have to forgive me, I've only got the one — is that the train doesn't like being remembered.

You read it. You remember what it said. The book becomes redundant. The train collects redundant things. That's all this is.

I have no evidence for any of that. But it sounds right when I say it out loud.

There's a book I read four carriages back. It burned in my hands before I could finish the last page.

I think about it more than I should. Not because the words were important. Because I'll never know what the last words were.

I write these knowing they'll burn after you read them.

That's the deal. I'm okay with it. If a thing has to be temporary to exist at all, then temporary is the form it takes.

Just — read it. Then let it go.

Tunnels are good for chests.

Nobody slows down in a tunnel. The light from your lantern moves with you and everything outside that lantern is decided afterwards. Push a chest into a corner of one and most people won't notice it on the way through.

I noticed. So now you've noticed.

Sometimes there are chests on the side of the tracks in the tunnels. Not on the train. Tucked against the tunnel wall, where the train doesn't reach.

They almost never have anything I'd call good. Pickaxes mostly. Spare rails. Shovels with the handles worn smooth.

Somebody built these tunnels. Somebody is still maintaining them. I try not to think about who.

This was an ice carriage. Whole car full of it, packed solid. I chipped this chest out with the pickaxe end of an axe I shouldn't have been using like that.

There's water under the floor now. I'm leaving before someone asks me about it.

Found a chest half-buried in mud. Couldn't tell if someone put it there or if the mud rose up around it.

Both options bothered me equally so I stopped thinking about it and just dug.

The moss carriages are the worst for finding chests. The moss is patient. It grows over hinges, over latches, sometimes over the whole lid until the chest looks like a green stone.

If you see a green stone in a moss carriage that isn't quite the right shape — that's a chest. Trust me. Or don't. You'll trip over the next one anyway.

Some carriages are built thin. Hallway-thin. Just enough room to walk and nothing else.

Look up. There's almost always a space above. Hatches, false ceilings, a foot or two of crawl. Half of what I've found on this train was found by looking up in a place that didn't seem to be hiding anything.

The other half was found by tripping.

I've started finding other people's notes.

Not many. Maybe one every few hours, or every few carriages — the distance between them is never regular. They turn up at campfires. Almost always at campfires. Like the writer needed to warm up before they could think clearly.

I'd say it makes me feel less alone. It doesn't. It just makes me feel like I'm slightly behind.

Always by a campfire. Always.

The campfires here heal you — I assume you know that by now, or you wouldn't have made it this far. So the writer is stopping where it's safe to think.

I do the same now. I write these by campfires too. That's why you found this one.

I might be following someone who's already dead.

I keep finding their notes by campfires, but I don't know if they're still ahead of me or if I'm just walking through a trail of things they left a long time ago.

The newest note I've found doesn't have a date. None of them do.

I wonder if someone is following my notes the way I'm following theirs.

If you are — and I have no real reason to think you are not — hello. The campfire's a good place to read this. Wait until the fire's settled. Don't rush.

You'll learn the smell.

It's not the smell of a campfire. It's not the smell of wood. It's the smell of words evaporating, if you'll let me get poetic for a second.

After a while you stop noticing. Then you smell it on yourself and realise you've been carrying it for days.

I read the next one as slowly as I could. One word at a time, mouthing each one.

It still burned at the end. But it burned more slowly, in a way that felt — and I am almost certain about this — almost grateful.

I don't know what to do with that information.

I found a book and didn't open it.

I carried it for two days, in my pack, and every time I thought about it I felt a kind of itch. Not on my skin. Inside the thought.

On day three I read it. I had to. I don't know if the book made me, or if I made me. I suspect they were the same thing by that point.

There's a moment, right after a book finishes burning, where you can still see the page. Not on the floor. In your eye. Like a window has been closed and your eye remembers the shape of the light.

If you blink, you lose it. If you don't blink, your eyes water. Either way it's gone in under a minute.

I have started reading the last page twice.

I tried reading one to a campfire. Out loud.

The book still burned at the end, but I think the campfire heard. The flame leaned a different way for a moment. Maybe I imagined it.

I have nothing else to base this on. Try it. Tell me what you see.

I know their handwriting now. I knew it before I knew their name. Then I realised they hadn't given me a name in any of the notes I've found.

That bothered me for a day. Then I realised I've never given anyone mine either.

Found one tonight that still had the warm-paper smell. Like it had been written in the last hour.

I sat by the same campfire and waited. Nothing came. I'm not sure what I expected.

Eventually I wrote this and left, in the hope that whoever I'm chasing might do the same thing someday and find me by accident instead of effort.

There are no more.

Whatever they were leaving, this was the last one. I've checked the next three campfires. Nothing.

Either they made it out — and I don't believe in that — or this is where they stopped. I'm taking the last note with me. It deserves a witness.

I thought I'd lost the trail. Two days, no notes.

Then today, by a campfire I almost didn't bother stopping at, there it was. Same handwriting. Same way of crossing the sevens.

They're alive. Or someone is. I'm not asking too many questions about which.

I disagree with the one who wrote before me.

They think the slimes are worth fighting. They are not. I've left a note next to theirs saying so. Whoever finds this last gets to decide.

Disagreement is a kind of company too, I think.

Some say they see echoes
of past lives here.

I don't really know
what that means,
and I don't think
I want to.

We all have echoes.
Even if we can't see them.

They live in us,
until we die.

Then.. they are free.

Are these passengers alive? Or just drones? It can be hard to tell. Sometimes they don't seem quite ... right?

They are easily befriended none the less.

They all like food, but if you give them some armour or tools, that's the quickest way to get some friendly allies in this uneasy place.
